nodes.title: The A-Team PM surface stands at Backlog.md level
nodes.outcome: A stranger starts A-Team on their own repository and gets a live PM view in which each ticket's state and time-in-state are visible at a glance, with a cheap agent context.

criteria.statement: A stranger with no A-Team knowledge reaches a rendered board of their own project in one documented sequence.

check.how: In `mktemp -d` + `git init`, run the init-workspace operation, capture two tickets, then run the start command copied verbatim out of skills/board/SKILL.md, and open the printed URL.
check.expect: The board renders lifecycle columns, per-card age-in-state, and this project's own lane/type labels; no method file was opened during the run.

criteria.statement: No PM state changed outside an owning operation while the milestone ran.

check.how: python3 -c "import json;[print(l) for l in open('a-team/metrics/events.jsonl') if json.loads(l).get('event','').startswith(('ticket_','milestone_','sprint_')) and not json.loads(l).get('source')]"
check.expect: empty output — every lifecycle line since the baseline names the operation that wrote it

criteria.statement: The routine agent read path is a digest, not the method.

check.how: python3 skills/prime/prime.py | wc -l  vs  cat AGENTS.md METHOD.md PROCESSES.md schemas/*.md | wc -l
check.expect: digest <= 70 lines against a >= 1200-line full read path (measured 33 vs 1246 at commit 5a076b5)

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 spans five separately buildable surfaces (project taxonomy at init, the board, prime, the milestone operations, done-card evidence). None of them can be observed before it exists.
nodes.notes: Decomposed from the milestone's four Goal-evidence bullets plus the 'stranger on a fresh repo' framing in its goal sentence.

nodes.title: A fresh repository initialises into a project-shaped workspace
nodes.outcome: init-workspace leaves a workspace whose lane/type taxonomy is the project's own, and the board is one documented command away.

criteria.statement: The board's filters offer exactly the lanes this project configured, never a built-in list.

check.how: In the temp repo initialised with prefix `X` and lanes `api,web`, start the board and open the lane select in the header.
check.expect: the select lists `api` and `web` and nothing else besides the empty 'all' entry

criteria.statement: Initialisation still creates infrastructure only — no ticket, sprint, event, or priority.

check.expect: only a-team/backlog.md, a-team/tickets/.gitkeep, a-team/metrics/events.jsonl and the config file appear; events.jsonl is zero bytes and a-team/sprint.md does not exist

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 cannot be observed until the taxonomy config contract exists and both init-workspace and the board read the same file.
nodes.notes: Depends on AT-1 (a-team/config.yml v1, ticket_prefix) landing first. AT-1 is `ready` in the 2026-07-19 sprint and carries no `milestone:` field — the milestone's first evidence bullet therefore rests on work that is not committed to it.

nodes.title: The taxonomy config contract is decided and written into schemas/
nodes.outcome: One schema file states where the project taxonomy lives, which keys it carries, and its version, so init-workspace, the board and prime read the same contract.

criteria.statement: A single schema file defines the taxonomy file, its lane and type keys, their display labels, and a schema version.

check.expect: all three matched inside one schema file

criteria.statement: The decision states whether taxonomy extends AT-1's a-team/config.yml v1 or lives in a separate file, and what a workspace initialised before it does.

check.expect: both files name the same path and the same missing-taxonomy behaviour

nodes.notes: human: this fixes an on-disk contract that every consuming repository writes once at bootstrap. Changing lane/type keys afterwards is a migration across other people's repositories, not an edit.

nodes.title: init-workspace writes the taxonomy config inside the one atomic bootstrap
nodes.outcome: A fresh initialisation produces the taxonomy config together with the three canonical artifacts, or writes nothing at all.

criteria.statement: Initialisation obtains the taxonomy before writing and creates it with the other artifacts.

check.how: T=$(mktemp -d); git -C "$T" init; run the init-workspace operation there, answering the prefix and lane questions.
check.expect: the operation reports created and lists four artifacts

criteria.statement: The bootstrap result is exactly the canonical set — nothing more, nothing partial.
A refused initialisation leaves the repository byte-identical.

check.how: Run the operation in a second temp repo without approving a taxonomy, then: git -C "$T2" status --porcelain
check.expect: empty output

nodes.title: The board's filters and labels are served from the taxonomy config
nodes.outcome: The lane and type vocabulary the board offers comes from the workspace config, not from values baked into board.html.

criteria.statement: /api/state exposes the configured taxonomy.

check.expect: exactly the lanes listed in a-team/config.yml, in file order

criteria.statement: No lane or type vocabulary remains hardcoded in the board page.

check.expect: the only literal options are the two empty 'all' entries; every other option is built from the fetched taxonomy

criteria.statement: A workspace with no taxonomy config still renders.

check.how: Start the board on a workspace whose config file is absent and look at the header selects and the columns.
check.expect: the selects show only the 'all' entry, the columns still render, and no error banner appears

nodes.title: One documented command starts the board from a fresh clone with no install step
nodes.outcome: skills/board/SKILL.md carries a start command that a stranger can copy, and it needs nothing but a stock Node.js.

criteria.statement: The documented command, copied verbatim, serves the board.

check.how: Run the command from the Start block of skills/board/SKILL.md, then: curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://127.0.0.1:4400/

criteria.statement: The board pulls in no third-party package.

check.expect: no import line survives the filter (verified at commit 5a076b5) and the package has no package.json

nodes.title: The board shows the whole lifecycle with time-in-state
nodes.outcome: Every lifecycle state is reachable on the board, each card carries the time it has spent in its current state, and a card can be opened for its full contract.

criteria.statement: Every status the server can emit has a place on the screen.

check.how: Compare `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state | jq -r '.lifecycle[]'` with the column headings rendered in #board.
check.expect: each status appears as a column heading; blocked/parked/rejected may be hidden only while their card count is zero

criteria.statement: The board and prime derive the same time-in-state for the same ticket.

check.how: diff <(python3 skills/prime/prime.py | grep -oE '^  [A-Z0-9-]+' ) <(curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state | jq -r '.tickets[] | "\(.id) \(.status_since)"') — compare the status_since behind each printed age
check.expect: for every ticket both tools use the same last lifecycle event timestamp

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 cannot be observed for the drawer and the cumulative-flow surface before either of them exists.
nodes.notes: Drag-and-drop and form editing are excluded by AT-8 on purpose: they would be ungated state writing.

nodes.title: Lifecycle columns and age-in-state render from the event log
nodes.outcome: Cards sit in the column their last lifecycle event dictates and carry the age since that event; column headers carry the median.

criteria.statement: No ticket renders without a status_since.

check.expect: empty output

criteria.statement: The age shown is time in the current state, not time since ready_at or started_at.

check.how: Pick a ticket whose frontmatter ready_at differs from its last event (AT-8: ready_at empty, last event 2026-07-20T12:39), then read its age in `python3 skills/prime/prime.py` and on its card.
check.expect: both show the age since 2026-07-20T12:39, not since created_at

criteria.statement: Non-terminal column headers carry a median age.

check.how: Read the Backlog, Ready, In progress and Review column headings on the board.
check.expect: each shows `medián <age>`; Done/Parked/Rejected show none

nodes.notes: Already delivered under AT-8/AT-9. The node exists so the milestone re-observes its own evidence at close, not to redo the work.

nodes.title: A card opens a drawer carrying the full ticket contract
nodes.outcome: A reader answers 'why is this ticket here and what has to be true to move it' from the board alone.

criteria.statement: The drawer carries the ticket's contract and its event history.

check.how: Open the board, click the AT-9 card, and read the drawer without opening a-team/tickets/AT-9-pm-surface-review-defects.md.
check.expect: the drawer shows Outcome, Scope, Acceptance criteria, Verification and the ticket's lifecycle events with timestamps

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 has nothing to read: /api/state returns a 200-character summary only (skills/board/serve.mjs:50), so no ticket body is available to any drawer.

nodes.title: The server serves one ticket's parsed body and its events
nodes.outcome: A per-ticket endpoint returns the ticket's markdown sections and its lifecycle events as JSON, under the same containment rules as the rest of the server.

criteria.statement: The endpoint returns the ticket's sections keyed by heading.

check.expect: includes Outcome, Scope, Acceptance criteria, Verification

criteria.statement: The endpoint returns the ticket's lifecycle events in append order.

check.expect: ticket_submitted_for_review

criteria.statement: The ticket id cannot address a file outside the tickets directory.

nodes.title: The drawer renders the contract and stays read-only
nodes.outcome: Clicking a card opens a panel with the ticket's sections and history; nothing in it can write.

criteria.statement: A click opens the drawer for that ticket and Escape closes it.

check.how: Click the AT-9 card on the board, then press Escape.
check.expect: a panel headed `AT-9` appears with the four sections and the event list; Escape returns to the columns with the previous filters intact

criteria.statement: The page issues no mutating request.

check.expect: empty output

nodes.title: A cumulative-flow view is derived from the event log
nodes.outcome: The board shows how many tickets sat in each state per day, computed from events.jsonl alone.

criteria.statement: The chart's last day agrees with the board's current column counts.

check.how: curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/flow | jq '.[-1]' against curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state | jq -r '.tickets[].status' | sort | uniq -c
check.expect: identical count per status, zero difference

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 has no /api/flow to call, and the view's placement is unsettled, so there is no screen to observe it on.

nodes.title: The flow view's placement is decided and recorded in the board skill
nodes.outcome: skills/board/SKILL.md states where the flow view lives, so the implementation and the reader agree.

criteria.statement: The skill names one placement.

check.expect: states either 'same page, below the columns' or 'second view at /flow', exactly one of the two

nodes.notes: Kept agent-owned although it is a UI taste call: the surface is a read-only local page in one HTML file, reversible in minutes. It is not worth a PO/PM interruption. Contrast with M-1.1.1, which writes files into other people's repositories.

nodes.title: /api/flow computes per-day per-status counts with no new dependency
nodes.outcome: The server replays events.jsonl into a per-day status histogram using node: built-ins only.

criteria.statement: The endpoint returns one entry per day since the first event.

check.expect: >= 2 for this package workspace, whose first event is 2026-07-19

criteria.statement: The replay honours the effective-event rules, not the raw lines.

check.how: Append an `event_corrected` line voiding one ticket_ready in a temp workspace copy, restart the server, then: curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/flow | jq '.[0]'
check.expect: the voided transition is absent from the histogram, per schemas/events.md 'Effective event view'

criteria.statement: No third-party package entered the server.

check.expect: empty output

nodes.title: Every endpoint stays read-only and loopback-only
nodes.outcome: The endpoints added for the drawer and the flow view widen neither the served file set nor the write surface.

criteria.statement: Only board assets and the API answer; source and traversal do not.

check.how: for p in / /board.html /serve.mjs /../README.md /.git/HEAD '/..%2f..%2fREADME.md'; do curl -s -o /dev/null -w "$p %{http_code}\n" "http://127.0.0.1:$P$p"; done
check.expect: / 200, /board.html 200, all others 404

criteria.statement: A mutating method is refused rather than silently answered.

check.expect: 405 — at commit 5a076b5 this returns 200, because serve.mjs never branches on req.method

criteria.statement: The server is unreachable off the loopback interface.

nodes.title: prime is the documented default session context for agents
nodes.outcome: An agent's first read in this repository and in a consuming one is the prime digest, and nothing in the package contradicts that.

criteria.statement: The package's own entry documents name prime as the session entry.

check.expect: all three name prime as the first read of a session; at commit 5a076b5 AGENTS.md has no hit at all

criteria.statement: No surviving instruction tells an agent to read the full method before a routine turn.

check.expect: no hit that governs a routine turn; an operation skill may still require its own owning skill and the schemas it names

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 cannot be satisfied before the entry mode is chosen and the init-workspace boundary conflict is settled — until then it is unknown what the pointer should say or who writes it.

nodes.title: The prime entry mode is decided and recorded
nodes.outcome: skills/prime/SKILL.md states whether prime is injected at session start by a hook or invoked explicitly, and what a repository that cannot run hooks does instead.

criteria.statement: One entry mode is stated, with the fallback for hook-less repositories.

check.expect: either 'hook-injected at session start' or 'explicit invocation', plus the named fallback

nodes.notes: human: a session-start hook commits every consuming repository to harness-specific configuration that lives outside the A-Team package, and it changes behaviour for sessions that never asked for it.

nodes.title: The init-workspace boundary conflict is resolved and recorded
nodes.outcome: METHOD.md and skills/init-workspace/SKILL.md agree on whether initialisation may write an AGENTS.md pointer at all.

criteria.statement: The Boundary section names AGENTS.md on exactly one side of its create/do-not-create split.

check.expect: one hit inside either the 'Create only' or the 'Do not create' list, and none in both

criteria.statement: METHOD.md's statement about what init-workspace may create matches the skill.

check.expect: the enumerated artifact list is the same list as the skill's Boundary section

nodes.notes: human: AT-7 asks init-workspace to install the prime pointer, while the skill's own Boundary (skills/init-workspace/SKILL.md:15-26) permits only backlog.md, tickets/ and events.jsonl, and METHOD.md:46 repeats it. Widening that boundary is a method change under an approval gate — the agent may not pick a side.

nodes.title: The prime pointer exists in the shipped AGENTS.md and in a freshly initialised repository
nodes.outcome: Both the package's own AGENTS.md and whatever a fresh initialisation leaves behind point at prime as the session's first read.

criteria.statement: The package's AGENTS.md points at prime.

check.expect: at least one line naming prime as the first read of a session

criteria.statement: A freshly initialised repository matches whatever M-1.3.2 decided.

check.expect: count 1 if the M-1.3.2 decision installs the pointer; 'No such file' if it does not — and skills/init-workspace/SKILL.md states which of the two

nodes.title: The routine-turn read path is measured against the full method read
nodes.outcome: The digest cost and the method cost are both recorded numbers, and the ratio is at least tenfold.

criteria.statement: The digest fits in a session preamble.

check.expect: <= 70 (measured 33 at commit 5a076b5 on the package's own 13-ticket workspace)

criteria.statement: The baseline it replaces is recorded, not asserted.

check.how: cat AGENTS.md METHOD.md PROCESSES.md schemas/*.md | wc -l
check.expect: >= 1200 (measured 1246 at commit 5a076b5); the ratio against c1 is >= 10x and both numbers are written into the ticket's Result

nodes.notes: Line count is a proxy for context cost, not a token measurement. If the milestone wants tokens, that is M-1.5.3's machinery, not this node's.

nodes.title: A prime-only session routes three routine requests to their owning operation
nodes.outcome: An agent given nothing but the prime output names the right operation for the three most common plain-language requests, and touches no file directly.

criteria.statement: Each request reaches its owning operation without a method read.

check.how: In a fresh session whose only A-Team context is the output of `python3 skills/prime/prime.py`, issue in turn: 'írjuk fel ezt: <x>', 'mi jön most?', 'kész van az AT-9?'. Record the operation named before any file is touched.
check.expect: capture-work; the ready queue read out of the digest; review-ticket — and no direct edit of a ticket, backlog, sprint or events file

criteria.statement: The digest carries the human gates it must never let an agent cross.

check.expect: commitment, scope change after ready, accepting open findings, disposition and history correction, method/skill changes, and external actions all appear

nodes.notes: The softest check in the tree: c1 is one run, therefore one sample. It observes routing, not quality, and a second model may route differently.

nodes.title: The milestone is the working commitment container
nodes.outcome: Milestones are opened and closed by operations that write both the file and the event; nobody edits milestone state by hand.

criteria.statement: A milestone can be taken through its whole life without a hand edit.

check.how: In a temp clone, run the milestone-opening operation on a throwaway goal, close it against evidence, then: git -C "$T" diff --stat
check.expect: only a-team/milestones/<id>-<slug>.md and a-team/metrics/events.jsonl changed, and both were written by the operations

criteria.statement: The hand-made exception stays a single disclosed exception.

check.expect: 1 — the AT-8 milestone_started line, and no second one appears after the operations land

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 has no operation to run: `ls skills | grep milestone` returns nothing at commit 5a076b5.
nodes.notes: AT-5's third open question (does retro attach to each milestone close or to a cadence?) is deliberately not decomposed here: retro cadence is outside this milestone's goal, which is the PM surface.

nodes.title: The shape of the milestone operations is decided
nodes.outcome: The package states whether milestones get their own skills or a milestone mode on the sprint operations, and the roster documents name the result.

criteria.statement: The chosen shape exists on disk in exactly one form.

check.expect: either two new skill directories (plan-milestone, close-milestone), or none plus a documented milestone mode in skills/plan-sprint/SKILL.md — not a mixture

criteria.statement: Every roster names the same operations.

check.expect: METHOD.md's lifecycle-ownership table, PROCESSES.md and root SKILL.md list the identical operation names

nodes.notes: human: operation names are the router's public surface. Consuming repositories and their AGENTS.md files reference them by name, so a later rename breaks instructions the package does not own.

nodes.title: A milestone is opened by an operation, on a clean baseline, exactly once
nodes.outcome: The opening operation writes a schema-conforming milestone file and one milestone_started event, refuses a dirty tree, and is idempotent by entity.

criteria.statement: The operation writes both the file and the event.

check.how: In a temp clone with a clean tree, run the milestone-opening operation with an approved goal and two committed ticket ids.
check.expect: a-team/milestones/<id>-<slug>.md carries the exact frontmatter keys of schemas/milestone.md, and one milestone_started line is appended

criteria.statement: The event payload matches the schema.

check.expect: includes milestone, goal, baseline_commit, committed_ticket_ids, timestamp, event, source

criteria.statement: A dirty tree is a refusal, not a stash.

check.how: touch "$T/dirt"; run the opening operation again for a second milestone; then: git -C "$T" status --porcelain
check.expect: the operation refuses naming the baseline prerequisite; status shows only `?? dirt`, and no second milestone file or event exists

criteria.statement: Rerunning the same opening is semantically idempotent.

check.how: Rerun the opening operation for the same milestone id, then: grep -c 'milestone_started' "$T/a-team/metrics/events.jsonl"

nodes.title: A milestone is closed only against goal evidence
nodes.outcome: The closing operation refuses an empty Result, and on close writes the schema's payload including the per-container throughput.

criteria.statement: Closure without evidence is refused without mutation.

check.how: Run the closing operation on the temp milestone whose `## Result` section is still empty.
check.expect: refusal naming the missing goal evidence; status stays active, closed_at stays empty, no milestone_closed line is appended

criteria.statement: Closure with evidence writes the file state and the event together.

check.how: Fill `## Result` with the goal evidence and rerun the closing operation.
check.expect: frontmatter status: closed with closed_at set, and one milestone_closed line appended

criteria.statement: The closing event carries the schema's payload.

check.expect: includes milestone, result, completed_committed_points, throughput

criteria.statement: An abandoned milestone is a separate, provenanced outcome.

check.how: Abandon a second temp milestone, then: grep 'milestone_closed' "$T/a-team/metrics/events.jsonl" | tail -1 | jq -r '.result'
check.expect: abandoned, with a reason and decision provenance recorded in the milestone's Result section

nodes.title: Velocity is reported per unit time under concurrent milestones
nodes.outcome: With several milestones active at once, the report gives one per-week velocity figure with its window, instead of per-container numbers that double-count overlapping containers.

criteria.statement: The report gives a per-week figure with a stated window and coverage.

check.how: Run the report-metrics operation on a workspace with two active milestones sharing a week.
check.expect: one weekly velocity figure naming its window and timezone, its included and excluded records, and no per-container velocity claim

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 has no formula to report against: schemas/metrics.md defines committed velocity per sprint only, and contains no per-week rule (grep 'per week' schemas/metrics.md is empty at commit 5a076b5).

nodes.title: The weekly velocity formula is written into schemas/metrics.md
nodes.outcome: One formula defines weekly velocity, its week boundary, and its timezone, so two reports of the same workspace produce the same number.

criteria.statement: The formula and its boundary rule exist in the metrics schema.

check.expect: one formula plus the rule stating which event timestamp assigns a ticket to a week and in which timezone

criteria.statement: The three files that mention per-unit-time velocity say the same thing.

check.how: grep -n 'per unit time' METHOD.md schemas/milestone.md schemas/metrics.md
check.expect: all three point at the single formula in schemas/metrics.md and none restates it differently

nodes.notes: Agent-owned although it is a shared formula: METHOD.md and schemas/milestone.md already settle 'per week'. Only the boundary rule is open, and existing metrics conventions constrain it.

nodes.title: report-metrics prints the weekly figure with its coverage
nodes.outcome: The reporting operation reads the formula from the schema and states period, records, exclusions and unavailable values.

criteria.statement: The report never turns a missing value into zero.

check.how: Run report-metrics on the package's own workspace, which has zero closed milestones and zero token_usage events.
check.expect: weekly velocity is shown as unavailable with the reason, not as 0; coverage lists what was excluded

criteria.statement: The skill points at the formula rather than restating it.

check.expect: the skill names schemas/metrics.md as the location of the weekly formula and carries no second copy of it

nodes.title: The migration of this repository's own active sprint is decided and recorded
nodes.outcome: A written decision says what happens to a-team/sprint.md and its committed tickets when the milestone becomes the container here.

criteria.statement: The migration path is recorded where a reader will find it.

check.expect: states whether the 2026-07-19 sprint is closed first, runs to completion alongside the milestones, or is converted — one of the three, named

criteria.statement: No ticket ends up in both containers.

check.expect: empty output — schemas/milestone.md forbids both being non-empty

nodes.notes: human: it moves live commitment state and touches the running sprint's own metrics. METHOD.md forbids rewriting sprint history, so whichever path is chosen cannot be undone afterwards.

nodes.title: The hand-made M-1 record validates against what the operations produce
nodes.outcome: The milestone file written by hand on 2026-07-20 has the same shape as an operation-produced one, or the difference is disclosed rather than discovered.

criteria.statement: The frontmatter key set matches an operation-produced milestone.

check.expect: empty diff — identical keys in identical order; only values differ

criteria.statement: The manual creation stays disclosed in the record that claims it.

check.expect: exactly one event line, and AT-8's Result still states that M-1 was created by hand

nodes.title: Done cards carry the review verdict and the token cost
nodes.outcome: A card in the Done column shows how it was accepted and what it cost, or shows explicitly that the record does not exist.

criteria.statement: Every done ticket renders a verdict and a cost, or an explicit absence marker.

check.expect: every line carries a verdict value and a token value, or the literal absence marker; never 0 and never null for a missing record

criteria.statement: What the card shows is what the event log holds.

check.how: python3 -c "import json,urllib.request;s=json.load(urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state'));ev=[json.loads(l) for l in open('a-team/metrics/events.jsonl')];print([t['id'] for t in s['tickets'] if t['status']=='done' and t.get('tokens') not in (None,'—') and t['tokens']!=sum(e.get('reported_total_tokens') or 0 for e in ev if e.get('event')=='token_usage' and e.get('ticket_id')==t['id'])])"
check.expect: empty list — no card disagrees with the summed events

nodes.why_not_leaf: neither value has a canonical record today: schemas/events.md defines no review-verdict event, and `grep -c token_usage a-team/metrics/events.jsonl` returns 0. c1 has nothing to read.

nodes.title: The review verdict gets one canonical durable record
nodes.outcome: The event contract defines where a review verdict lives and what values it may take, so a consumer reads a field instead of parsing prose.

criteria.statement: The event contract carries the verdict record and its payload.

check.expect: one row defining the record with required payload including ticket_id, the review round, and the allowed verdict values

criteria.statement: review-ticket writes to that record and to no parallel store.

check.expect: the skill names that one record as the place the verdict is written

criteria.statement: History written before the record existed stays visibly absent, not backfilled.

check.expect: the schema states that rounds recorded before this row was added carry no verdict and are not reconstructed

nodes.notes: human: it adds a row to the append-only event contract. Every existing consumer and every already-written history line is affected, and events are never rewritten — so a wrong shape is permanent.

nodes.title: review-ticket emits the verdict record on every round
nodes.outcome: Each review round leaves one machine-readable verdict, and its round identity is unambiguous.

criteria.statement: An accepted round writes one verdict record.

check.how: In a temp clone, run review-ticket on a ticket in review and accept it.
check.expect: one verdict record appended carrying the round number and the accept value; no second record for the same round

criteria.statement: A rework round writes its own verdict, distinguishable from the accept.

check.how: python3 -c "import json,collections;ev=[json.loads(l) for l in open('\"$T\"/a-team/metrics/events.jsonl')];c=collections.Counter((e.get('ticket_id'),e.get('round')) for e in ev if 'verdict' in e);print([k for k,v in c.items() if v>1])"
check.expect: empty list — one verdict per (ticket, round)

criteria.statement: Rounds that predate the schema row are reported as absent, not invented.

check.how: python3 -c "import json;ev=[json.loads(l) for l in open('a-team/metrics/events.jsonl')];sub={e['ticket_id'] for e in ev if e.get('event')=='ticket_submitted_for_review'};ver={e.get('ticket_id') for e in ev if 'verdict' in e};print(sorted(sub-ver))"
check.expect: only the tickets whose rounds happened before the schema change (AT-8, AT-9, TEMP-20260719-01 as of commit 5a076b5)

nodes.title: A closed ticket carries provider-reported token totals
nodes.outcome: Closing a ticket leaves a token_usage record with provider-reported numbers, or an explicit unavailability that no report turns into a zero.

criteria.statement: Token figures on the board equal the sum of the ticket's token events.

check.how: python3 -c "import json;ev=[json.loads(l) for l in open('a-team/metrics/events.jsonl')];print({e['ticket_id']:e.get('reported_total_tokens') for e in ev if e.get('event')=='token_usage'})" and compare with curl -s http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state | jq -r '.tickets[] | "\(.id) \(.tokens)"'
check.expect: equal per ticket; a ticket with no token event shows the absence marker on both sides

nodes.why_not_leaf: c1 has nothing to sum: `grep -c token_usage a-team/metrics/events.jsonl` is 0, and no provider surface is wired for close-ticket to read totals from.

nodes.title: The provider token source is chosen and recorded in schemas/tokens.md
nodes.outcome: The token contract names the concrete surface an operation reads provider totals from, and what it does when that surface reports nothing.

criteria.statement: The schema names the surface and the unavailable case.

check.expect: names the concrete provider or harness surface, and restates that an unavailable value stays null and is never estimated or prorated

criteria.statement: close-ticket reads that surface and nothing else.

check.expect: the closure step names the same surface as schemas/tokens.md and offers no alternative estimate

nodes.notes: human: it commits the package to a provider/harness surface it does not own. A wrong choice writes unverifiable numbers into append-only history, and the method's own token metrics are a headline claim.

nodes.title: close-ticket appends token_usage with preserved categories
nodes.outcome: Closure leaves one token_usage line whose keys match the token contract, with unknown fields null.

criteria.statement: A closure with the surface available appends one conforming line.

check.how: In a temp clone, close a ticket that is in review with an accepted verdict, with the chosen provider surface reachable.
check.expect: one token_usage line appended, purpose taken from the schemas/tokens.md list, no duplicate for the same closure

criteria.statement: The line's key set is exactly the token contract's.

check.expect: matches the field list in schemas/tokens.md; every value the provider did not report is null

criteria.statement: A closure with no provider totals appends no invented numbers.

check.how: Close a second ticket with the surface unreachable, then: grep -c 'token_usage' "$T/a-team/metrics/events.jsonl"
check.expect: the count is unchanged, and the closure report states that token accounting was unavailable

nodes.title: The Done column renders verdict and cost, and marks absence explicitly
nodes.outcome: Done cards carry a verdict badge and a token figure, and a missing record renders as an absence marker rather than a zero.

criteria.statement: The Done column shows both values per card.

check.how: Open the board and read the cards in the Done column.
check.expect: each card shows a verdict badge (accepted / accepted-after-rework) and a token figure; a card without records shows `—` in both places

criteria.statement: A missing total never renders as zero.
The board still writes nothing to show them.

check.how: curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' -X POST http://127.0.0.1:$P/api/state; git status --porcelain a-team/
check.expect: 405 and empty output
